title: The Second Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## February 25, 2024 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

## LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Second Sunday in Lent falls on February 25, 2024, placing this sermon six days before Ash Wednesday (which occurred on February 14, 2024—meaning Lent was already underway). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, this Sunday's readings typically include Genesis 17:1-7, 15-16 (God's covenant with Abram and Sarai), Psalm 22:23-31, Romans 4:13-25 (faith of Abraham), and Mark 8:31-38 (Jesus predicts his passion). The liturgical focus centers on covenant, faith tested through trials, and the call to self-denial and cross-bearing as disciples.

## EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in early 2024 was navigating post-General Convention (2022) implementation of various resolutions, with particular attention to ongoing questions about sexuality and inclusion that had dominated church discourse. In the Diocese of Colorado, Bishop Kee Sloan (who began serving in 2020) was leading the diocese through these denominational transitions. No major diocesan crises or bishop elections were occurring at this specific moment, though the larger Anglican Communion remained fractured over LGBTQ+ inclusion, with the Church of England's recent decisions on same-sex blessings (approved in February 2023) continuing to reverberate.

## DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical late-winter weather in late February 2024, with temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit and no major winter storms reported in the immediate week prior. Locally, attention was on the Colorado legislature's 2024 session (which began in early January and would run through mid-May), with debates over education funding, housing affordability, and mental health resources. No major breaking news events specific to Capitol Hill or downtown Denver were dominating headlines in the immediate week before February 25.

## NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the 2024 presidential campaign intensifying, with Super Tuesday primaries scheduled for March 5, 2024 (less than two weeks away). Former President Donald Trump was consolidating support in the Republican primary after Iowa and New Hampshire victories, while President Joe Biden faced some concern among Democratic insiders about his viability, though he had won early contests. The House of Representatives was engaged in budget negotiations and debates over foreign aid (particularly Ukraine funding), with Congress deadlocked over supplemental spending bills. Additionally, the economy remained a central concern with interest rates high and inflation discussions ongoing.

## INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The war in Ukraine was in its second year, with winter conditions affecting military operations and humanitarian needs intensifying as Russia maintained territorial gains in eastern regions. In the Middle East, tensions were high following Israel's military operations in Gaza (which had escalated dramatically after October 7, 2023), with humanitarian crises and ceasefire negotiations receiving international attention. China's political tensions with Taiwan and ongoing restrictions on religious freedom in mainland China were also part of the broader geopolitical picture, though not dominating Western headlines in this specific week.

## CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in late February 2024 was in the pre-Oscars period (the 96th Academy Awards would air on March 10), with film industry attention on major nominees and awards season celebrations. The zeitgeist was marked by election-year polarization and fatigue, economic anxiety among middle-class Americans despite headline job numbers, and ongoing national divisions over social issues including education and reproductive rights. The mood was somewhat anxious and divided, with many Americans expressing concern about the direction of the country and the choice between presidential candidates; religious communities like the Episcopal Church were navigating these cultural tensions while trying to maintain prophetic witness and pastoral care.
